3 - 1MM Trophy Talk - James Swanwick and Adrian Nicholls.mp4 Thank you. Hello, everyone. So this was December 30th, 2023, 13 months ago. And this is a whiteboard in a... Medellin, Colombia hotel boardroom that Adrian and I were in and we were planning out the year that was to come that just finished. And. You can see here, this is the end of 2023, October, November, December. You can see we did $113,000 revenue. in October, $206,000 in November, and $196,500 in December. That's of 2023. And then when we were in this boardroom a year ago, we said... Let's aim for a million dollar month and let's see if we can do it by the end of 2024. And we actually circled here. So this one million figure we wrote on the board on December 30th, 2023. Now, September of this year, we got close, as Jeremy said. We got to 949,000. Then October, we actually went backwards. We dropped 17. 6%. We went back to 782,000. We made a couple of mistakes. Then we came back again in November. We got to $875,000 cash collected in Stripe. And then, literally, on December 30th, we crossed a million dollar month. And if you have a look at this, December 30th, 2024, which was only a couple weeks ago, this is actually the moment that we realized that we just crossed it. We were on a Zoom call having an exec meeting. This is Adrian, me, and our COO, Becca. And at 5:22 p. m., Adrian said, 'We just made another sale. We just crossed a million.' And if you look at this here, December 30th, 2023, we said, 'Let's make a million dollar month.' And on December 30th, literally 365 days later, we actually crossed a million dollars. So I don't know if you guys believe in manifestation, but... That's pretty powerful. So let me just tell you a little bit about what we do. I'm going to give you some general lessons that I have learned over the years because I've been in this business now almost 10 years. So I'm going to give you some general lessons of how we got here. And Adrian's going to come up once I've finished here, and he's going to go into some more specific things like software we use, call rates, show rates, cost per acquisition, things like that. He's going to get into the more nitty gritty. So we essentially help entrepreneurs and executives to stop drinking. This is a screenshot of a recent Zoom call of a lot of our clients. And this is our main product, Project 90. It's a 90-day stop drinking program. It costs $15,000. We have a couple of down sales. One of them is just... $170 program, do it yourself. The other downsell is a 5K coaching program with less bells and whistles than the main product. Then we've got a couple of upsells. One that's $12,500 and the other one that's $6,000. On the front end, our average order value is $10,500. The main funnel... in 2024 was literally a Facebook or a YouTube ad to a VSL to book a call and then a two-call close. These are the ads, some of the Facebook ads we ran. And in fact, I've just got some of the more recent ones. Those who were at Jeremy's Q4 meetup. You'll notice the house in the background, and he's got his red car there. I also walked out here about three months ago, and I shot a video with one of the boats out here in the background. This is a Fast Company article, which gives us some social proof. And, you know, these are just three examples of some of our ads, all going to this VSL page. This is the top part above the fold of our VSL page. It's just got a headline. It's got a video. Underneath the video, there's a type form application. where the prospect would click. that then go through. I think it's like a 14 or 15 question type form. We really make them jump through hoops. Then they end up booking a call and then it redirects to a confirmation page. We give them some homework. We try to encourage them to go through that homework before they get on to their first call. We send them some testimonials, all the basic marketing 101 stuff here. We've got some social proofs, some magazines, TV shows we've featured on. A lot of testimonials. We then send them text message reminders before their call. I'm sure every marketer here is doing this. But all of these things combined really has helped us get the show rate up. Now, in 2024, that main simple VSL funnel... Generated. $6. 8 million in revenue. Literally, an ad to a VSL to a book a call to a two-call close. The most disgusting thing about our funnel, I think, is that we haven't changed that video. We haven't tested that video in like eight months. So I'm sure that we can optimize that. We just haven't had a CRO person. So, as bad as that VSL is, because we haven't tested it, it still generated $6. 8 million in 2024. But just... 10 years ago. This whole organization was just a crapy little $67 program, barely selling anything. Just for context, I stopped drinking in 2010 just for lifestyle reasons. I was living in LA in the Hollywood Hills at the time and a lot of these people were saying, 'Oh, is it true that you don't drink? How do you have such a good time and you're not drinking?' And I said, 'I don't know. I just have a good time and I don't drink.' And about five years later, or four years later, I was being mentored by Tai Lopez. This is on October 3rd, 2014. That's my mum on the left. That's my... who's Ty's cousin, Ty Lopez there, and then some of you guys might know Mark Dahmer. This was literally 10 years and three months ago. And we were up at Ty's place, and I said to Mark one afternoon, I said, 'I got this idea for a stop drinking program.' And he said, 'Let's go down to Sunset Boulevard to the hotel and we'll map it out.' And we went down, I think it was the Andaz Hotel there, which is just down the road from where Ty was living in the hills. And we just got a... Hotel, napkin, and we sketched out the first iteration of what would become the 30-day no alcohol challenge. Now, I didn't know how much I should charge for it, but Ty was just... about to come out with the 67 steps and he was charging $67. So I thought, 'Alright, $67 to stop drinking for 30 days' and I started this program. You know, it did modestly well. I started trying to sell it on YouTube channels. This is Brandon Carter here, from nine years ago. Me and him were hanging out. Brandon's just over here in the corner. This is how long I've been doing this. You know, right in the beginning, we're just trying to, like, get the word out there about this stop drinking program. Simultaneously, I had this idea for a blue-blocking company. So I actually started two companies at the same time: this stop drinking program and this blue-blocker company. Thank you. And the Blue Blocker company went like this. We did a million dollars in sales in the first year. So it put a lot of my energy into that. And not so much energy into the stop drinking program. Remember this is 2015 now, okay. Now, this is what our revenue has been in the stop drinking business over the years. So you can see here when I started it off as just a lifestyle business, I only generated $8,500 in 2015. Then I jumped up to $154,000 in 2016, $200,000 in 2017. This is a year, by the way. This is not a month. Now, simultaneously, I was growing this blue blocking business. I was learning stuff from Tai Lopez on sales. I was traveling around. I wasn't really giving this my full attention. Candidly, it was just a lifestyle business. 2018, $189,000. 2019, half a million. 2024, $ 55. 2021. I started taking a little bit more seriously. I kind of let my brother run the blue blocker. I came back into this business, did 854K in 2021, $1. 2 million in 2022. Adrian came on board halfway through 2023. We did $2 million in 2023, and then last year we more than tripled, went to $6.8 million. Now, if we stay on this path, we're projected to do at least $14 million this year and maybe $20 million next year. But who knows? We'll see how we go. But you can see here, I've been doing this for 10 years. It's not like I started this two years ago and just it was a rocket ship. I was distracted. I was doing other things. It was a lifestyle business. I went and lived in Bali and was shirtless driving a moped around. I wasn't really focused on this as a business until a few years ago. So, like I said, I'm going to give you a few general lessons that I've learned over the years. And these might seem so simplistic that you guys might not feel you're getting anything out of it. But I think it's important to go over. With these general lessons, because a lot of us are looking for that one funnel, that one breakthrough, that one hack. And yes, there's a lot of those lessons, and Adrian's going to talk about those in just a minute, but the general lessons I feel like we really need to get into our mindset here. Simple. Persistence, time, and grit. I've been in this business for 10 years, helping people stop drinking. Admittedly, not full-time for 10 years. Part-time for probably six or seven. Full-time for three. But just the persistence to keep going. There are many times I'm like, 'Ah, bugger this business.' This is too hard, right? Especially during COVID. And I was sitting in Brisbane, Australia in lockdown. And I'm trying to. I'm doing the sales calls myself and there are no shows. Getting up at six in the morning to talk to someone in California, they don't show up. I'm like, 'Why am I getting up this early in the morning to do this stuff?' Like, I really wanted to chuck the business away, but I stuck with it. I'm really happy that I did. Coaches, mentors, masterminds, just like all of us here. I know this is simplistic, but it's a huge component of why I feel we've had a lot of success. Adrian and I just went to a Tony Robbins seminar, Date with Destiny, just up the road here in West Palm Beach, only three or four weeks ago. We invest in our personal development. So I've done landmark education. I've done landmark forum. I have a landmark coach every week. Adrian invests in his own personal development. I pay for some of the staff in our... organization to get personal development. We have a psychologist and a therapist in-house who's there to support people whenever they want support, whether it's to do with work or whether it's their personal lives. So we take personal development very, very seriously, and that has definitely contributed to our growth. This is something I learned from Tai Lopez. Always be testing. Always, always be testing. He who tests most wins. So over the years, I've tested pricing. was $67. Fast forward to 10 years, it's $15,000. But I wouldn't have known that unless I was in a mastermind, not dissimilar to this one, where someone said to me, 'You should price high. You should make this thousands of dollars, not less than $100.' The demographic, we're always testing the demographic. In the beginning, I was trying to help everyone stop drinking. And I had modest success. Then, when I changed the demographic to 'I'm going to support entrepreneurs, executives over the age of 40 who've got enough money to spend thousands of dollars on solving this problem,' the business went like this. So I stopped trying to help everyone and I started trying to help just the demographic that I most wanted to work with. Always be polishing the product, obviously. I think it was Ryan who was speaking last quarter here, and he was telling us about how we made $50 million in revenue. The product sort of fell off the cliff, right? And that caused all of those kind of problems. So with us, we've always been ruthlessly focused on how do we make the product even better, because we do not want cancellations, we don't want refund rates. So we're obsessed with that. For me, I have to say sticking to one offer or industry has been really terrific. Now, for me, I had the Blue Blockers business and I had the Stop Drinking business. And I would submit most— people who know me in the online marketing world would probably know me for one of those two things— but I see a lot of marketers bouncing from one offer, one product, one thing to the other. I think that it loses the impact of the messaging then. But for 10 years, most people, at least in these types of rooms, have known me as, like, the Australian guy, the blue blocker guy, the stop drinking guy. And I think that's really helped me to attract a lot of help over the years from marketers and from mentors and advisors. Likewise, build a brand, not just a product. In the beginning, I had this product, right, the 30-day no-alcohol challenge. And then years later, I turned this into a brand, which is now called Alcohol-Free Lifestyle. That's our logo on the bottom right. And now we have five different offers, okay? So it's not just one product. It's now a brand. I mentioned this just before, and I am personally known for one or two things. I seem to be known for being the Australian guy, the blue blocker guy, the stop drinking guy. I met Justin before, and, you know, I was saying to him, like, I know... him from social media because of his attire, like, he's well-dressed. Right, that's a brand recognition I don't exactly know what he does or how he supports people, but I know of him. So, I think, for you and us as marketers, if we can be known for one thing or one style or one whatever that really is going to attract a lot of people. Some more specific lessons. This has been a huge thing for our business, which is price high and justify. Like I was saying in the beginning, we had a 30-day no alcohol challenge. It was $67. And then, the moment that we changed it to... $3,500, then $4,500, then $5,500, all the way up to what it is now, $15,000, and just justify that additional price point. Wow, you've got so many more marketing dollars to be able to invest, right? But when you're trying to... make things cheap or you're trying to give discounts, it's like a race to the bottom. So I like to price high and justify. And I would just invite all of you, like whatever your prices are, just think about: could you double those prices? And how would you justify the doubling of that? Get comfortable with paid advertising and trust your advisors, obviously. Now, when I met Jeremy, we were doing maybe $10,000, most $15,000 a month in ad spend. Now we're doing a quarter of a million dollars a month in ad spend. But... I had to really get comfortable with that. When you're first doing it, you're like, 'Oh, I'm not sure about this.' But then you see the math actually works. As long as the math works and you trust your advisor, then go for it. Jeremy. You're going to spend... Oh, thank you. Yeah, I didn't realize. I... Thank you. It's a lot of awards points, huh? Record, edit, and supply new ads consistently. This was something that I learned from Tai Lopez when I was mentored by him. I saw him all the time bringing out his iPhone. shooting ads all the time, all the time, all the time. I can't tell you how many marketers I've met over the years who are like, 'God, I try to get the founder and the owner to give me some ads. It takes three weeks, four weeks, six weeks. They get around to it like once a quarter. They give me six ads and they think that's great. Whereas I'm just like, I pull out the iPhone and I just shoot the ads. And I saw that from watching Ty do 24 iterations of his, 'Hi, I'm just here in my garage video.' I mean, that was like the 24th version of that. And before that, he drove around the Hollywood Hills. He filmed himself doing a version of that ad. But the one that popped was the one in front of his Lamborghini in the library up in the Hollywood Hills. wasn't he just had the idea and he shot one ad he went and shot 24 ads and that was the one that popped so I took that lesson and so Jason here is who's now does our YouTube and Google Ads would probably say that he's probably got a year's worth of ads from me already sitting in a library. Like literally I've got hundreds of ads that I have recorded over the years. They're not asking me for new ad creative because there's already like hundreds of them sitting in a library. Make sense? Hire A players only. I mean, in the early iterations of the business, I was just trying to save money. I was hiring people in the Philippines. I met a guy in Bali. I was paying... like $1,500 a month to run my ads. He did a pretty good job. He did a good job for where the business was. But I was like, 'Yes, I'm only paying this guy $1,500 a month.' But then the moment I had a mindset shift and I started hiring A players, such as Adrian here, when Adrian came in 18 months ago and Becca Rowland here, some of you guys may have met Becca a couple of months. Masterminds ago. So many companies have gone downhill because of like little factions breaking out, irritations, resentment. We just cut all that crap out of our business as best as we can. Hire people who have 10 years more experience than you or who've generated 10x more money than you. Because again, I was trying to save money by hiring people who were cheap instead of hiring people who were the best at what they did. Jeremy Haynes is an example of that. How I actually came to know about Jeremy and then ended up hiring him as a consultant, I was in Medellin, Colombia. I was sitting in a hotel. And this guy came up to me because he saw my pair of Swannies blue blockers. Where are they? Yeah, I just had my pair of Swanee's. And he goes, 'Oh, Swanee, I like that brand.' I've got a pair of those back at home. I go, 'Oh, that's interesting.' And he goes, 'Yeah, yeah, they're great.' What's your name? I said, 'James Swanee.' And he went, 'Oh, wait, you're the blue blocker guy. You own that company.' I said, 'Yeah.' He goes, 'Oh, that's amazing.' What else are you doing? And I said, 'Oh, I've got this stop drinking business.' And he said. 'Oh, like I Am A Comeback. Yeah, yeah, Jeremy Haynes helped them do like $20 million in revenue in 18 months.' And I was sitting there listening, just trying to play it cool. Inside, I was like, 'Damn, that's amazing.' And I Am A Comeback is kind of like a friendly competitor. Not really, but like I knew of them. I was thinking, 'Okay.' I said, 'What's that guy's name again?' He said, 'Yeah, Jeremy Haynes.' I'm like, 'Oh, okay, cool.' Anyway, I said, 'See you later.' He walked off. I quickly pulled out my Instagram. I'm like typing in Jeremy Haynes. And I saw that a friend of mine, Mark Dahmer, followed him. You know, I was showing you Mark Dahmer up on the screen before. So I quickly then WhatsApped Mark Dahmer, and you can see this, October 26, 2023. Mark, do you know Jeremy Haynes? He apparently helped IAM a comeback, and I'd like to speak with him and maybe hire him as a consultant. Mark said, 'Yeah, I know him well.' Want me to introduce you? And then you can see down below, I said, 'is he a good guy?' Thank you. He told me that he was. He was all right. The next day, I was on the phone with Jeremy. I was in an Uber between Medellin, Colombia, and somewhere, I'm not sure, towards Perea, which is a town outside of the thing, talking to Jeremy. Jeremy on the phone. And I think within 72 hours, if I recall correctly, I hired Jeremy as a consultant. And he's been instrumental in helping us think bigger, push. Also, looking at what was our existing funnel. And one of the big things that Jeremy advised us to do was, when people were filling out the type form, we were actually having them click, go to another page, and then fill out the questionnaire and book a call. But Jeremy said, 'No, just have it on the same page as the VSL.' And that simple change was enough for our conversion rate to drop considerably, or rather, the cost per acquisition, to drop considerably, I should say. So again, just coming back to the main point of that, hire people who have 10 years more experience than you or who have generated 10x more money than you. So for me, when I came to meet Jeremy, even though... I hadn't met him in person. I'm in Colombia. He's here. I looked at it and I went, 'Okay, this guy seems to have generated 10 times more money than I have, or at least for my competitor, I am a comeback.' So that was like a simple hire. Hire an operations manager and a COO. This is Becca, who's our COO, and she's amazing. And that really has been an important hire because, you know, I treated it like a lifestyle business for many years. But when Becca came in, she looked at it, and Adrian, when Adrian came in as well, looked at it and went, 'Okay, this has to stop being a lifestyle business.' Where we're like taking people's credit cards and sharing the info and WhatsApp groups, and we've got to move the software here. We've got to get like official here. We're doing all this kind of stuff. That's like a lot lifestyle, business kind of systems and operations. Becker came in and probably went, 'My God,' and then changed a whole lot of stuff. And now we're running as an eight-figure enterprise. Playing defense as well as offense. A lot of folks in here, myself included, over the years are always like, 'Grow, scale, let's go, come on, faster.' But thinking about playing defense has really helped us immensely. I lost our Facebook ad account during the pandemic for six months. I didn't have a legit script set. We couldn't do much or couldn't do anything in paid advertising. Now we have at least two backup Facebook ad accounts. Credit card processes. Stripe is somewhat screwing us. At the moment. We have very, very small refunds, but it's enough for Stripe to withhold money from us. They're saving about $100,000 from us at the moment. Holding back $100,000 of our money until we kind of sort out some of these very, very few refund requests or cancellations. A second credit card processor in case we go down on Stripe has been important, imperative, I would say. We hired a lawyer. I was like, 'I don't to hire a lawyer that costs so much money,' but we hired this lawyer, she's great, she's $250 an hour, but we send her stuff to do with staff agreements, we hired an FTC compliance expert who came in and looked at all of our ads, looked at all of our funnels, made sure that we were FTC compliant because the absolute worst thing that I think could happen to us is the FTC investigates us. comes up, slows us down, you know, shuts us down. I don't want any of that kind of stuff. I'm really, really grateful to all the folks who speak at Jeremy's events here where they tell us their horror stories about mistakes that they've made. There was a gentleman a few masterminds ago who got sued by Nike. Do you guys remember that talk? And as a result of that, I was sitting there going, 'Damn, we need insurance. We need business insurance in case we ever get sued.' So we went out and we got business insurance. Now, thankfully, we haven't had to use it. And I hope we never do. But I tell you what, I sleep a lot better at night knowing that I've got it. Stay on top of fulfillment. Be obsessed with your client's success. Obviously, I do personalized welcome videos for every new client, and it has destroyed— cancellation and refund requests. Here's an example. I recorded in Zoom. On the right-hand side here, I literally filmed those walking through the streets of Miami yesterday. So I think we had four, maybe five, one, two, three, I had five new clients yesterday at like 15K a pop. and I just walk there and I do this little one-minute video. It says, 'Hi, Bill, James Swanick here.' I saw you joined Project 90. I just wanted to welcome you to the community. It's great to have you with us. I really admire you. for embarking on this alcohol-free lifestyle adventure. Just a big piece of advice. Stay really engaged throughout the process. Get to know the other clients. When you do that, it creates camaraderie. That leads to accountability. That will help generate the breakthrough that you want. I can't tell you how many of these new clients message back and go, 'Oh my God. Thank you so much.' I love the video. Thank you so much. I feel really, really good about this. It absolutely destroyed cancellations and refund requests. A personalized video. We're all so obsessed with automations where someone becomes a new client, we send them a not particularly personalized email. Congratulations, welcome. But when you send a video from the founder who they've seen on a Facebook ad—someone they've been watching and Googling and investigating before they buy— when they get a video like this within hours of them enrolling, wow, now they feel really safe and secure that they've made the right decision. part ways with people not in alignment with the company vision. I had a sales guy who was with me for three years. Let's call him Jim. He was amazing. He was instrumental in helping me take the price of the program from $2,500 to $5,000 to $10,000. To 15 to 20, we even tried 20 and he just went for it and I loved that and he was. He still is a great friend of mine. But when Adrian came in and looked at our processes and systems and started changing things, and I empowered Adrian to come in and change the systems and processes in the best interest of the future growth of the business. Jim did not like that. Jim was resentful. Jim didn't do things. Jim didn't get on board the new way. And we've learned as a company, it's so challenging to get... people to change their behavior. It's so much easier to bring new people in who just assimilate with the behavior, but trying to like teach an old dog new tricks was really challenging and sadly we parted ways. And this guy was with me for three years, was instrumental in the business for three years. But we parted ways, and not on great terms, sadly. Since then, we've kissed and made up, and everything's good. But. just had to go. The new culture came in, this is where we're going, you're either getting on the train or you're getting off the train. We've been building the business to scale and sell, so I don't want one-on-one coaching. I only want group. I only want something that scales. Wayne Gretzky's talking about skate to where the puck is going. Everything that we're doing today when we're sub 10 million is designed to where we're going to be when we're doing 20 million. So I'm thinking, not that I want to sell it right now. I may not ever want to sell it because it's a great cash-producing business. But we're building it in a way now to sell it if that time comes. And in doing so, we actually have amazing systems and processes, great onboarding, great offboarding processes, and we create the best product. Uh. Foster radical honesty and conscious communication. Adrian's on the executive team. Becca's on the executive team. We all read this book together, The Five Dysfunctions of a Team. Audio books we all gave ourselves two weeks to go over it and then we met in a boardroom and we literally discussed the book and what came out of that is us having radical conscious communication. So, if Adrian sees something that I'm doing that he thinks is inefficient, or he feels like he needs to pull me up because I spoke to someone poorly, he will say it. Because he has the confidence to say it, because we've gone through the five dysfunctions of a team. We've all sat around and had incredibly awkward, uncomfortable conversations with each other. Incredibly awkward and uncomfortable. We've had four people on a call where three of us are talking to one person who's... Whose responsibilities or whose performances dropped. And that person, I can just see that person— just like, oh, but it's just radical, radical honesty. Conscious communication within a comfortable space. Does that make sense? And in doing that, you destroy resentment, you destroy infighting, you just... destroy people doing things that ordinarily they might not do. This is my summary of the book, like we share it in a Slack channel. This is literally like the exec channel where we write down the notes and we share it with each other. This is another book I read called Never Lead Alone by Keith Ferrazzi, who's a great friend and an amazing mentor, who's amazing at helping build company culture and team culture within organizations. Again, this is a book review. I've written out my notes, and I've shared it with my executive team, and we talk about the books together. Get to know your staff personally and support them personally. So around Christmas time, I realized I'd never met most of the Philippines-based staff. Like we have VAs, we've got tech people. I kind of saw their names and I kind of saw their profile photo, but I'd never met them, never had a conversation with them. And I said, you know what, that's it. I'm going to make 25 minutes available to all of them and I get on a call and I'm going to get to know them. Ends. Wow, it was amazing what they said and what they shared, how they felt when the CEO and founder was literally taking time to have a 25-minute Zoom conversation with them. About their lives. I was asking about their dog's name, their mother's name, their children's name, what they do personally. We talked about business maybe for five minutes of the 25 minutes and the 20 minutes was just about them personally. And the amount of goodwill that that seemed to generate was profound. I got a message here from Crystal over in the Philippines. I gave them all a little bonus, $350. Like, not much in the broad scope of the business, but huge for someone in the Philippines. And look at her saying, like, 'I'm truly surrounded by great people that supports and appreciates my work.' Heart is so genuine for helping not just us but a lot of people. May God shower you with more blessings and abundance, etc. etc. Like this is the kind of praise and love that you get from your team when you just take the time to get to know them. Here's another example: this is a woman called Tina on our team. I just wanted to send a quick message to say Thank you for the Christmas bonus and your incredible generosity, your kindness and the value you bring to us. VAs means so much. It's truly an honor to work with you and everyone on the AFL team. Thank you again. et cetera, et cetera. It doesn't take much. It took me a couple of hours in the last few days before Christmas to just reach out to them, have a conversation. But the goodwill that it created was profound. Also, these are a couple of our sales folks. I bought one of our sales guys a pair of awesome headphones for Christmas, and I actually bought Aston Villa English soccer team tickets. For Sam and his girlfriend, Christina, and I, we sent them to Birmingham, England, to go and watch his favorite football team, because he was doing such a good job. So again, these little things— personal touches— create incredible, strong relationships, and all of that combined creates an incredible company culture which keeps us on track. So I'm going to just invite Adrian to come up now, because Adrian joined us in in the summer of 2023, I think it was about 18 months ago, and came into what was probably a lifestyle business, right? With lifestyle operations and lifestyle systems. And he oversaw the changing of the guard, so to speak, and helped turn the direction of the company. In a way now that's leading us to being like a pretty sizable, significant enterprise. But I think around 2021 or 22, Adrian was what Jeremy... Jeremy Mott, what, sorry, what Jeremy Mott referred to as a brokey, weren't you Adrian? Fair enough, yeah. Yeah, pretty much a brokie. But yeah, I'd done pretty well previously, but as we all know, sometimes you go through a bit of a rough patch. I got stuck out in Brazil. during COVID and my coaching business went to zero. I was there for a year and a half in Brazil. And then when I go back to Bali, I had pretty much what it says here. I'd borrowed $250 off a mate just to get by. I couldn't even put fuel in my scooter. But I think what I wanted to go into here is the mindset. Down here, I had like $250 to my name, but the mindset was, you know, I'm going to come back strong. Um. That's when I met James out in Bali. I got on a high-ticket offer. I'd never done high-ticket sales before. I'd had a coaching business. I got a background in NLP. And I got onto a real estate offer. And in two years, I was able to do $5 million closed over the phone. Never done it before. In that business, what I did is I'd watch the whole operation. We went from $80,000 a month when I got on the offer. There was a sales leader that I took over, and I'd become the closer there. But I watched the whole business go from $80,000 a month to the $1. 2 million a month. James asked me, 'When was it?' It was April of, around April of 2023, I jumped on James' offer and, well, I think I did a call for you and... I did the call and I hung up the phone. I'm like, 'How do I do a follow-up?' He didn't have a CRM system. It's just, you know, call and then I'm like, 'Where do I put the credit card?' He's like, 'Just put it in a WhatsApp chat.' And there's this WhatsApp chat of the whole team and there's credit cards going into the... into the WhatsApp chat. So I looked at it and then I pitched him and said, 'Look. You don't have any systems.' We need to build out better systems. But at the time, he was doing like $230,000 a month. So he was like, 'But why?' We're doing $230,000 a month. What do we need systems for? What I really want to get into is the mindset. Here in 2023, he was doing about $180,000 a month. But when I did these notes, you know, AFL eight-figure build. I'd never done this before, by the way. I've never built out a business. But I'd watched what we'd done in the other offer. I'd watched from afar. And I just really want to wrap our heads around mindset and how important it is just to really think big. You've got to bet on yourself when your current circumstances aren't where you want them to be. Decide what you want. Um. When you decide what you want and where you're going, it makes it easier to say no. If you're building your business, give away the parties. Whatever you're doing, decide what you want and then just push the rest to the side. Practicing gratitude, like when you're back at 250 bucks in the bank account. Anything from there, like your first paycheck, you're pretty grateful for. Show up every day, solve problems and find the solutions. Always be building relationships. I met James. We met in Bali. I think we'd done a walk on the beach for your birthday. A year later, he'd asked me to close a deal for him. Thank you. And then, a year later, I jumped onto the team. So, just always always building relationships, think big, believe in your vision, speak—uh— speak into it daily. It's something super important: operate at a higher frequency, and then be mindful of the mindset of other people in the business in other positions. Because we had another team member doing the, you know, just for one example, we had a guy doing our Facebook ads. He was only spending $5,000 a month. A five thousand dollar a month mindset— so everything we come to him with, like, 'Hey, try this,' 'Let's do this for yourself,' 'Let's do that.' His mindset would always counteract it, and we couldn't grow. So you've got to be mindful of the people in your team and their mindsets. The second one will be systems. So when we come into James' business, there was no systems. There was nothing. We, because I observed it, I just wrote down pretty much everything we had to do and we started to create better systems because without systems you just can't scale. Can we see here? I can't get a better view. Um, here's a perfect system that we built out. So something super important when it comes to sales is no friction. You want to be able to move through. When you take money... uh, from a client, you want to be able to move through the process as fast as possible. You want to get like with the videos that James does here. We want to get the stickiness as quick as possible because, within those first... you know, 20, 30 minutes to an hour, they're thinking about all the reasons why they have just done what they've done. So a system we built out here is in our Slack channel in the... in the enrollers channel. We call it the closers channel. We make it super easy for the closers to be able to just move from one step to the other. They've got their daily workflow. They take payment. They then send the agreement on the phone. They then book in the onboarding call. Then they do a new member background. And then James sends the video. And this happens like clockwork. So when I flew up yesterday, we did five sales in a day. And as a sales manager, I didn't talk to the team once. And we did $100,000 in revenue yesterday. So these types of systems just make it seamless and prevent the refunds. So with systems, build strong systems, SOPs, communications, and sales processes. Ensure flawless onboarding. Yeah, before I came in, it was super clunky and I was going to a WhatsApp chat. Has he got the agreement? Has he not got the agreement? And then all of a sudden you're getting refunds. So ensure a flawless onboarding system, payment gateways agreements in the first hour. Automate where possible to eliminate any energy drainers. So I was at dinner the other night with a couple of big closers. I live down in Medellin, Colombia, and I'm at dinner. And I was asking these guys, 'What are other offers out there like?' And they said, 'They're not like your offer.' Like, you know, they've got the closers doing the SMSs, they're calling out, they're emailing, doing no-shows follow-up. Our systems are so dialed that... The closes are pretty lucky, as Jeremy calls them, fat cat closes. They just sit back, the calls come in, and they're able to just focus on being in the calls and not have to worry about... Anything else? Simplicity in processes leads to efficiency and team mental bandwidth. So we just want to make sure we're— everybody in your team— we're conserving their bandwidth however we can. Sales. What was that off? We just came off systems. So sales. What we do in the team is we run a leaderboard in Slack. So we're always putting a bit of a competitive edge to the sales team. So they're always seeing each other's numbers. They know who the top sales guy is. They know the percentages. Everything is there for everybody to see. So there's no hiding, which is always helpful. We track everything in our, we've got a. I don't know this guy's is uh phenomenal— he builds out these spreadsheets anything spreadsheet we want he'll build them out for us— but this is a spreadsheet we run. We've got every single metric across the whole funnel, all the way to offer, close rate. The KPI is 21% deal to close, which means every deal that lands in the closes calendar. We're closing at 25% so every four deals that includes no shows cancels. We know that every single deal that gets in the calendar. We're going to close one in four which is pretty good for a 15k to 25k offer. When I come into James' business or when we got started, one thing I noticed... Actually, raise your hands. Who is doing sales now? Who does your closing calls? None of you guys have closes? Sorry, who does the sales calls yourselves? Yeah, and who has a team? And then who has a two call close? Okay, cool. Um. When it comes to resources, like... When I came into James' business, there was no clear path. People were coming through the funnel. It wasn't clear where they were going. They could click out of the funnel at any time. So they'd go into a Google Doc. or they'd go into one of the tools that we had in the homework page, and we're getting too far off the path. So this was back in 2023 when we came in. Improvements we made straight away was like a clear buyer's path, clear videos. All the stuff that most people are doing these days, financial calculator, building authority, we put in a university study into the homework page to give that authority as well. So we must have a clear buyer's path as we move through the funnel. So our sales process is a two-call close. This works super well on this offer. Like the first call is, it's 15 minutes. What we found was: we had originally 15-minute slots, but the show waits. Rates were horrible, we moved it to a 30-minute slot through Calendly. What we find is people see a lot more value in a 30-minute slot so they're going to show up. The show rates actually went up significantly by putting a 30-minute slot there in the for that first initial consultation. So, 30-minute slot: the call is only 15 minutes. And the call is basically just rubbing salt on their wound. So we really just... We just jump on the call. We ask them what's going on with the pain. We don't give them any solution and then we recommend them to get on that second call. What we do is we allow the resources to do all the heavy lifting for us. They'll land on a homework page. Watch a video. We've got a financial calculator. We've got a frequently asked questions. We've got a university study of the program. And then we've got all the testimonials and case studies. But what we really do is... it's a 15 minute call but we have the triage like literally walk them through every single step on that homework page like can you click on the link for me what do you see like every single step they're covering so when they get on that closer call The resources have done all the heavy lifting. Okay, we're indoctrinating them through a 20-minute, like as Jeremy said, with an hour of content. We hit them with 25 minutes of content. The second call... What I found works really well off the last offer I was on. To get down there. To bring down their sales guard, to bring down the guard, because people come on the call knowing they're going to be on a sales call. So what we do is we frame it, we just say, 'G'day John,' it's Adrian here. I'm actually a director of memberships. I never, I don't usually get on a lot of these calls. I work with James on the leadership team and instantly their guard goes down. They feel like they're getting an exclusive call. So, all of my directors, all of my guys, are directors of memberships. They've all got their own little thing to say. But since we say that their sales guard goes down and instantly they their influence goes up from the closer, because they're no longer closer now they're the director of memberships. They barely get on the calls. So we found that to be like super powerful. So, whatever offer you guys are on, have your sales guys say that or something similar. You know, I'm actually, you know, I'm John's right-hand man. Don't usually jump on a lot of these calls. So we do a two-call close triage. 15 minutes. As I said, Schedule is a 30-minute meeting. Then we go into a 45-minute meeting with an hour. We use, I got trained on this on my last offer. I didn't know it was, but this is very similar to Cole Gordon's method. On the sales call, we want to go through the seven beliefs. And these are the seven beliefs they need to have to buy. It's either seven reasons why they won't buy, or seven reasons or beliefs they need why they will buy. There must be pain, okay? We've got to have pain. They need to doubt that they can't do it on their own. So how we do that? We say, 'Why don't you just do it on your own?' 'Why don't you just quit drinking on your own?' Why don't you just do a 30-day challenge? So we need to get in their mind. That they're doubtful. They can't do it on their own. So in their mind, they're convincing themselves. So the time we come to the end of the sales call. It's simply just a layup. So there must be pain. We then move into doubt. They must doubt they can't do it. There has to be a cost. So there has to be a cost of inaction. If they don't do something about it, like what is it going to cost them further on? They need a desire. There needs to be a bigger goal. They need to have the money. They need to have support from a partner or a spouse or a business partner. And then they need to trust the company. Is the seven beliefs that they need to have so? The last on a second last section so scaling when it comes to scaling you need to look for the who and not the how. For us, it was Becca in operations. She was the one that was going to be helping us. To scale out this picture on the right hand side is everything I was doing at one stage back in 2023. It just got so busy that I had to say to James, 'I'm doing all this stuff. We need. We need to find the who, which was Becca. Thank you. So. To scale, create. The scalable infrastructure to get the growth. Focus on the small details because, at scale, once you leave the stratosphere at a million a month, smallest little things turn into really big problems. Find the who. The who knows the how. Consistency in execution every day keeps the business moving forward and then always be testing. So just recently, we launched a master class just to clean out the pipeline every month. Three months ago, we launched the masterclass, just a webinar with James, an executive session with James. And we're adding about 100K in extra revenue just driving everyone to this masterclass. Culture has been a really big one; so our sales team we are super dialed with our culture like our team they're all friends they're all um They're all helping one another. They're getting on role-playing with another. There's competition, but they're not against each other. So culture's been a massive one for us, above and beyond. They're always giving calls to one another. So that's been a super... That's been something that's really helped us with the growth. And then when it comes to culture. We focus on what's called a G7 mentality, like a game seven mentality in the NBA, which means like it's game seven, be able to trust everybody else on the team. They can take the shot so we have like a G7 mentality whenever we, whenever I bring anyone on, we've got 15 on the sales team now. Whenever I bring anyone in, I really i push Push towards them. It's a game seven mentality. You need to know that you can pass it to your teammate and they're going to be able to take the shot and get it in. So do the reps. Protect the culture at all costs. We had a pretty negative sales guy on the team. He was super entitled. You just got to get rid of the negativity straight away and just keep the energy high. Invest in your team. Always share the vision where you're going. And I'm always labeling our team as world-class. Like it's a world-class standard, world-class, world-class. They appreciate that. but they really lift their game and we just weed out anyone that's not a culture fit and they'll just they'll just fall away when it comes to sales meetings I don't introduce anyone into sales team for at least two weeks i'll have like we've got two meetings going over the week we have a ramp up team and then we have our our solid team you never want to you don't want to disrupt your culture and their performance because a players do not want to be on the same call as a b player or a c player everyone wants to be as an a player encourage collaboration accountability and then ongoing development. would be what we put together. So, yeah. Do you want to do some Q&A? Yeah, so thanks very much, everyone. Appreciate that. You want to just come up here?
